The hind that would be mated by the lion
Must die for love. 'Twas pretty, though plague,
To see him every hour; to sit and draw
His arched brows, his hawking eye, his curls,
In our heart's table; heart too capable
Of every line and trick of his sweet favour:
But now he's gone, and my idolatrous fancy
Must sanctify his reliques. Who comes here?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
O, were that all! I think not on my father;
And these great tears grace his remembrance more
Than those I shed for him. What was he like?
I have forgot him: my imagination
Carries no favour in't but Bertram's.
I am undone: there is no living, none,
If Bertram be away. 'Twere all one
That I should love a bright particular star
And think to wed it, he is so above me:
In his bright radiance and collateral light
Must I be comforted, not in his sphere.

Enter PAROLLES

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
[Aside] One that goes with him: I love him for his
sake;
And yet I know him a notorious liar,
Think him a great way fool, solely a coward;
Yet these fixed evils sit so fit in him,
That they take place, when virtue's steely bones
Look bleak i' the cold wind: withal, full oft we see
Cold wisdom waiting on superfluous folly.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Save you, fair queen!

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: And you, monarch!

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: No.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: And no.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Are you meditating on virginity?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Ay. You have some stain of soldier in you: let me
ask you a question. Man is enemy to virginity; how
may we barricado it against him?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Ay. You have some stain of soldier in you: let me
ask you a question. Man is enemy to virginity; how
may we barricado it against him?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Ay. You have some stain of soldier in you: let me
ask you a question. Man is enemy to virginity; how
may we barricado it against him?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Keep him out.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
But he assails; and our virginity, though valiant,
in the defence yet is weak: unfold to us some
warlike resistance.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
But he assails; and our virginity, though valiant,
in the defence yet is weak: unfold to us some
warlike resistance.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
There is none: man, sitting down before you, will
undermine you and blow you up.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
There is none: man, sitting down before you, will
undermine you and blow you up.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Bless our poor virginity from underminers and
blowers up! Is there no military policy, how
virgins might blow up men?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Bless our poor virginity from underminers and
blowers up! Is there no military policy, how
virgins might blow up men?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
Virginity being blown down, man will quicklier be
blown up: marry, in blowing him down again, with
the breach yourselves made, you lose your city. It
is not politic in the commonwealth of nature to
preserve virginity. Loss of virginity is rational
increase and there was never virgin got till
virginity was first lost. That you were made of is
metal to make virgins. Virginity by being once lost
may be ten times found; by being ever kept, it is
ever lost: 'tis too cold a companion; away with 't!

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
I will stand for 't a little, though therefore I die a
virgin.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
There's little can be said in 't; 'tis against the
rule of nature. To speak on the part of virginity,
is to accuse your mothers; which is most infallible
disobedience. He that hangs himself is a virgin:
virginity murders itself and should be buried in
highways out of all sanctified limit, as a desperate
offendress against nature. Virginity breeds mites,
much like a cheese; consumes itself to the very
paring, and so dies with feeding his own stomach.
Besides, virginity is peevish, proud, idle, made of
self-love, which is the most inhibited sin in the
canon. Keep it not; you cannot choose but lose
by't: out with 't! within ten year it will make
itself ten, which is a goodly increase; and the
principal itself not much the worse: away with 't!

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: How might one do, sir, to lose it to her own liking?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
Let me see: marry, ill, to like him that ne'er it
likes. 'Tis a commodity will lose the gloss with
lying; the longer kept, the less worth: off with 't
while 'tis vendible; answer the time of request.
Virginity, like an old courtier, wears her cap out
of fashion: richly suited, but unsuitable: just
like the brooch and the tooth-pick, which wear not
now. Your date is better in your pie and your
porridge than in your cheek; and your virginity,
your old virginity, is like one of our French
withered pears, it looks ill, it eats drily; marry,
'tis a withered pear; it was formerly better;
marry, yet 'tis a withered pear: will you anything with
it?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
Let me see: marry, ill, to like him that ne'er it
likes. 'Tis a commodity will lose the gloss with
lying; the longer kept, the less worth: off with 't
while 'tis vendible; answer the time of request.
Virginity, like an old courtier, wears her cap out
of fashion: richly suited, but unsuitable: just
like the brooch and the tooth-pick, which wear not
now. Your date is better in your pie and your
porridge than in your cheek; and your virginity,
your old virginity, is like one of our French
withered pears, it looks ill, it eats drily; marry,
'tis a withered pear; it was formerly better;
marry, yet 'tis a withered pear: will you anything with
it?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Not my virginity yet—
There shall your master have a thousand loves,
A mother and a mistress and a friend,
A phoenix, captain and an enemy,
A guide, a goddess, and a sovereign,
A counsellor, a traitress, and a dear;
His humble ambition, proud humility,
His jarring concord, and his discord dulcet,
His faith, his sweet disaster ; with a world
Of pretty, fond, adoptious christendoms,
That blinking Cupid gossips. Now shall he—
I know not what he shall. God send him well!
The court's a learning place, and he is one—

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: What one, i' faith?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: That I wish well. 'Tis pity—

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: That I wish well. 'Tis pity—

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: What's pity?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
That wishing well had not a body in't,
Which might be felt; that we, the poorer born,
Whose baser stars do shut us up in wishes,
Might with effects of them follow our friends,
And show what we alone must think, which never
Return us thanks.

Enter Page

sentences.speaker: PAGE
sentences.text: Monsieur Parolles, my lord calls for you.
Exit

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
Little Helen, farewell; if I can remember thee, I
will think of thee at court.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
Little Helen, farewell; if I can remember thee, I
will think of thee at court.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Monsieur Parolles, you were born under a charitable
star.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Under Mars, I.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: I especially think, under Mars.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Why under Mars?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
The wars have so kept you under that you must needs
be born under Mars.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: When he was predominant.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: When he was retrograde, I think, rather.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Why think you so?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: You go so much backward when you fight.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: That's for advantage.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
So is running away, when fear proposes the safety;
but the composition that your valour and fear makes
in you is a virtue of a good wing, and I like the wear
well.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
So is running away, when fear proposes the safety;
but the composition that your valour and fear makes
in you is a virtue of a good wing, and I like the wear
well.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
I am so full of businesses, I cannot answer thee
acutely. I will return perfect courtier; in the
which, my instruction shall serve to naturalize
thee, so thou wilt be capable of a courtier's
counsel and understand what advice shall thrust upon
thee; else thou diest in thine unthankfulness, and
thine ignorance makes thee away: farewell. When
thou hast leisure, say thy prayers; when thou hast
none, remember thy friends; get thee a good husband,
and use him as he uses thee; so, farewell.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Our remedies oft in ourselves do lie,
Which we ascribe to heaven: the fated sky
Gives us free scope, only doth backward pull
Our slow designs when we ourselves are dull.
What power is it which mounts my love so high,
That makes me see, and cannot feed mine eye?
The mightiest space in fortune nature brings
To join like likes and kiss like native things.
Impossible be strange attempts to those
That weigh their pains in sense and do suppose
What hath been cannot be: who ever strove
To show her merit, that did miss her love?
The king's disease—my project may deceive me,
But my intents are fix'd and will not leave me.

Flourish of cornets. Enter the KING of France, with letters, and divers Attendants
Flourish of cornets. Enter the KING of France, with letters, and divers Attendants

sentences.speaker: KING
KING
FIRST LORD
sentences.text: So 'tis reported, sir.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Nay, 'tis most credible; we here received it
A certainty, vouch'd from our cousin Austria,
With caution that the Florentine will move us
For speedy aid; wherein our dearest friend
Prejudicates the business and would seem
To have us make denial.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Nay, 'tis most credible; we here received it
A certainty, vouch'd from our cousin Austria,
With caution that the Florentine will move us
For speedy aid; wherein our dearest friend
Prejudicates the business and would seem
To have us make denial.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Nay, 'tis most credible; we here received it
A certainty, vouch'd from our cousin Austria,
With caution that the Florentine will move us
For speedy aid; wherein our dearest friend
Prejudicates the business and would seem
To have us make denial.

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text:
His love and wisdom,
Approved so to your majesty, may plead
For amplest credence.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
He hath arm'd our answer,
And Florence is denied before he comes:
Yet, for our gentlemen that mean to see
The Tuscan service, freely have they leave
To stand on either part.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:

And Florence is denied before he comes:
Yet, for our gentlemen that mean to see
The Tuscan service, freely have they leave
To stand on either part.

sentences.speaker: KING

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text:
It well may serve
A nursery to our gentry, who are sick
For breathing and exploit.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text: What's he comes here?
Enter BERTRAM, LAFEU, and PAROLLES

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text:
It is the Count Rousillon, my good lord,
Young Bertram.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Youth, thou bear'st thy father's face;
Frank nature, rather curious than in haste,
Hath well composed thee. Thy father's moral parts
Mayst thou inherit too! Welcome to Paris.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Youth, thou bear'st thy father's face;
Frank nature, rather curious than in haste,
Hath well composed thee. Thy father's moral parts
Mayst thou inherit too! Welcome to Paris.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Youth, thou bear'st thy father's face;
Frank nature, rather curious than in haste,
Hath well composed thee. Thy father's moral parts
Mayst thou inherit too! Welcome to Paris.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Youth, thou bear'st thy father's face;
Frank nature, rather curious than in haste,
Hath well composed thee. Thy father's moral parts
Mayst thou inherit too! Welcome to Paris.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: My thanks and duty are your majesty's.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
I would I had that corporal soundness now,
As when thy father and myself in friendship
First tried our soldiership! He did look far
Into the service of the time and was
Discipled of the bravest: he lasted long;
But on us both did haggish age steal on
And wore us out of act. It much repairs me
To talk of your good father. In his youth
He had the wit which I can well observe
To-day in our young lords; but they may jest
Till their own scorn return to them unnoted
Ere they can hide their levity in honour;
So like a courtier, contempt nor bitterness
Were in his pride or sharpness; if they were,
His equal had awaked them, and his honour,
Clock to itself, knew the true minute when
Exception bid him speak, and at this time
His tongue obey'd his hand: who were below him
He used as creatures of another place
And bow'd his eminent top to their low ranks,
Making them proud of his humility,
In their poor praise he humbled. Such a man
Might be a copy to these younger times;
Which, follow'd well, would demonstrate them now
But goers backward.

Exception bid him speak, and at this time
His tongue obey'd his hand: who were below him
He used as creatures of another place
And bow'd his eminent top to their low ranks,
Making them proud of his humility,
In their poor praise he humbled. Such a man
Might be a copy to these younger times;
Which, follow'd well, would demonstrate them now
But goers backward.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
I would I had that corporal soundness now,
As when thy father and myself in friendship
First tried our soldiership! He did look far
Into the service of the time and was
Discipled of the bravest: he lasted long;
But on us both did haggish age steal on
And wore us out of act. It much repairs me
To talk of your good father. In his youth
He had the wit which I can well observe
To-day in our young lords; but they may jest
Till their own scorn return to them unnoted
Ere they can hide their levity in honour;
So like a courtier, contempt nor bitterness
Were in his pride or sharpness; if they were,
His equal had awaked them, and his honour,

But goers backward.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
His good remembrance, sir,
Lies richer in your thoughts than on his tomb;
So in approof lives not his epitaph
As in your royal speech.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Would I were with him! He would always say—
Methinks I hear him now; his plausive words
He scatter'd not in ears, but grafted them,
To grow there and to bear, —'Let me not live,'—
This his good melancholy oft began,
On the catastrophe and heel of pastime,
When it was out, —'Let me not live,' quoth he,
'After my flame lacks oil, to be the snuff
Of younger spirits, whose apprehensive senses
All but new things disdain; whose judgments are
Mere fathers of their garments; whose constancies
Expire before their fashions.' This he wish'd;
I after him do after him wish too,
Since I nor wax nor honey can bring home,
I quickly were dissolved from my hive,
To give some labourers room.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text:
You are loved, sir:
They that least lend it you shall lack you first.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text:
You are loved, sir:
They that least lend it you shall lack you first.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
I fill a place, I know't. How long is't, count,
Since the physician at your father's died?
He was much famed.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: Some six months since, my lord.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
If he were living, I would try him yet.
Lend me an arm; the rest have worn me out
With several applications; nature and sickness
Debate it at their leisure. Welcome, count;
My son's no dearer.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: Thank your majesty.
Exeunt. Flourish
Exeunt. Flourish
Enter COUNTESS, Steward, and Clown

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: I will now hear; what say you of this gentlewoman?

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: I will now hear; what say you of this gentlewoman?

sentences.speaker: STEWARD
sentences.text:
Madam, the care I have had to even your content, I
wish might be found in the calendar of my past
endeavours; for then we wound our modesty and make
foul the clearness of our deservings, when of
ourselves we publish them.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
What does this knave here? Get you gone, sirrah:
the complaints I have heard of you I do not all
believe: 'tis my slowness that I do not; for I know
you lack not folly to commit them, and have ability
enough to make such knaveries yours.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text: 'Tis not unknown to you, madam, I am a poor fellow.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Well, sir.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
No, madam, 'tis not so well that I am poor, though
many of the rich are damned: but, if I may have
your ladyship's good will to go to the world, Isbel
the woman and I will do as we may.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
No, madam, 'tis not so well that I am poor, though
many of the rich are damned: but, if I may have
your ladyship's good will to go to the world, Isbel
the woman and I will do as we may.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Wilt thou needs be a beggar?

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text: I do beg your good will in this case.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: In what case?

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
In Isbel's case and mine own. Service is no
heritage: and I think I shall never have the
blessing of God till I have issue o' my body; for
they say barnes are blessings.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
In Isbel's case and mine own. Service is no
heritage: and I think I shall never have the
blessing of God till I have issue o' my body; for
they say barnes are blessings.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Tell me thy reason why thou wilt marry.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
My poor body, madam, requires it: I am driven on
by the flesh; and he must needs go that the devil
drives.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
My poor body, madam, requires it: I am driven on
by the flesh; and he must needs go that the devil
drives.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Is this all your worship's reason?

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
Faith, madam, I have other holy reasons such as they
are.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: May the world know them?

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
I have been, madam, a wicked creature, as you and
all flesh and blood are; and, indeed, I do marry
that I may repent.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
I have been, madam, a wicked creature, as you and
all flesh and blood are; and, indeed, I do marry
that I may repent.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Thy marriage, sooner than thy wickedness.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
I am out o' friends, madam; and I hope to have
friends for my wife's sake.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Such friends are thine enemies, knave.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
You're shallow, madam, in great friends; for the
knaves come to do that for me which I am aweary of.
He that ears my land spares my team and gives me
leave to in the crop; if I be his cuckold, he's my
drudge: he that comforts my wife is the cherisher
of my flesh and blood; he that cherishes my flesh
and blood loves my flesh and blood; he that loves my
flesh and blood is my friend: ergo, he that kisses
my wife is my friend. If men could be contented to
be what they are, there were no fear in marriage;
for young Charbon the Puritan and old Poysam the
Papist, howsome'er their hearts are severed in
religion, their heads are both one; they may jowl
horns together, like any deer i' the herd.

flesh and blood is my friend: ergo, he that kisses
my wife is my friend. If men could be contented to
be what they are, there were no fear in marriage;
for young Charbon the Puritan and old Poysam the
Papist, howsome'er their hearts are severed in
religion, their heads are both one; they may jowl
horns together, like any deer i' the herd.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
You're shallow, madam, in great friends; for the
knaves come to do that for me which I am aweary of.
He that ears my land spares my team and gives me
leave to in the crop; if I be his cuckold, he's my
drudge: he that comforts my wife is the cherisher
of my flesh and blood; he that cherishes my flesh
and blood loves my flesh and blood; he that loves my
flesh and blood is my friend: ergo, he that kisses
my wife is my friend. If men could be contented to
be what they are, there were no fear in marriage;
for young Charbon the Puritan and old Poysam the
Papist, howsome'er their hearts are severed in
religion, their heads are both one; they may jowl
horns together, like any deer i' the herd.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Wilt thou ever be a foul-mouthed and calumnious knave?

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
A prophet I, madam; and I speak the truth the next
way:
For I the ballad will repeat,
Which men full true shall find;
Your marriage comes by destiny,
Your cuckoo sings by kind.

sentences.text: Get you gone, sir; I'll talk with you more anon.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Get you gone, sir; I'll talk with you more anon.

sentences.speaker: STEWARD
sentences.text:
May it please you, madam, that he bid Helen come to
you: of her I am to speak.

sentences.speaker: STEWARD
sentences.text:
May it please you, madam, that he bid Helen come to
you: of her I am to speak.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
Sirrah, tell my gentlewoman I would speak with her;
Helen, I mean.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
Sirrah, tell my gentlewoman I would speak with her;
Helen, I mean.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
Was this fair face the cause, quoth she,
Why the Grecians sacked Troy?
Fond done, done fond,
Was this King Priam's joy?
With that she sighed as she stood,
With that she sighed as she stood,
And gave this sentence then;
Among nine bad if one be good,
Among nine bad if one be good,
There's yet one good in ten.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: What, one good in ten? you corrupt the song, sirrah.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: What, one good in ten? you corrupt the song, sirrah.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
One good woman in ten, madam; which is a purifying
o' the song: would God would serve the world so all
the year! we'ld find no fault with the tithe-woman,
if I were the parson. One in ten, quoth a'! An we
might have a good woman born but one every blazing
star, or at an earthquake, 'twould mend the lottery
well : a man may draw his heart out, ere a' pluck
one.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: You'll be gone, sir knave, and do as I command you.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
That man should be at woman's command, and yet no
hurt done! Though honesty be no puritan, yet it
will do no hurt; it will wear the surplice of
humility over the black gown of a big heart. I am
going, forsooth: the business is for Helen to come
hither.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Well, now.

sentences.speaker: STEWARD
sentences.text: I know, madam, you love your gentlewoman entirely.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
Faith, I do: her father bequeathed her to me; and
she herself, without other advantage, may lawfully
make title to as much love as she finds: there is
more owing her than is paid; and more shall be paid
her than she'll demand.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: STEWARD
sentences.text:
Madam, I was very late more near her than I think
she wished me: alone she was, and did communicate
to herself her own words to her own ears; she
thought, I dare vow for her, they touched not any
stranger sense. Her matter was, she loved your son:
Fortune, she said, was no goddess, that had put
such difference betwixt their two estates; Love no
god, that would not extend his might, only where
qualities were level; Dian no queen of virgins, that
would suffer her poor knight surprised, without
rescue in the first assault or ransom afterward.
This she delivered in the most bitter touch of
sorrow that e'er I heard virgin exclaim in: which I
held my duty speedily to acquaint you withal;
sithence, in the loss that may happen, it concerns
you something to know it.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
You have discharged this honestly; keep it to
yourself: many likelihoods informed me of this
before, which hung so tottering in the balance that
I could neither believe nor misdoubt. Pray you,
leave me: stall this in your bosom; and I thank you
for your honest care: I will speak with you further
anon.

Enter HELENA

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
Even so it was with me when I was young:
If ever we are nature's, these are ours; this thorn
Doth to our rose of youth rightly belong;
Our blood to us, this to our blood is born;
It is the show and seal of nature's truth,
Where love's strong passion is impress'd in youth:
By our remembrances of days foregone,
Such were our faults, or then we thought them none.
Her eye is sick on't: I observe her now.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: What is your pleasure, madam?

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
You know, Helen,
I am a mother to you.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: Mine honourable mistress.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
Nay, a mother:
Why not a mother? When I said 'a mother,'
Methought you saw a serpent: what's in 'mother,'
That you start at it? I say, I am your mother;
And put you in the catalogue of those
That were enwombed mine: 'tis often seen
Adoption strives with nature and choice breeds
A native slip to us from foreign seeds:
You ne'er oppress'd me with a mother's groan,
Yet I express to you a mother's care:
God's mercy, maiden! does it curd thy blood
To say I am thy mother? What's the matter,
That this distemper'd messenger of wet,
The many-colour'd Iris, rounds thine eye?
Why? that you are my daughter?

That you start at it? I say, I am your mother;
And put you in the catalogue of those
That were enwombed mine: 'tis often seen
Adoption strives with nature and choice breeds
A native slip to us from foreign seeds:
You ne'er oppress'd me with a mother's groan,
Yet I express to you a mother's care:
God's mercy, maiden! does it curd thy blood
To say I am thy mother? What's the matter,
That this distemper'd messenger of wet,
The many-colour'd Iris, rounds thine eye?
Why? that you are my daughter?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: That I am not.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: I say, I am your mother.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Pardon, madam;
The Count Rousillon cannot be my brother:
I am from humble, he from honour'd name;
No note upon my parents, his all noble:
My master, my dear lord he is; and I
His servant live, and will his vassal die:
He must not be my brother.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Nor I your mother?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
You are my mother, madam; would you were,—
So that my lord your son were not my brother,—
Indeed my mother! or were you both our mothers,
I care no more for than I do for heaven,
So I were not his sister. Can't no other,
But, I your daughter, he must be my brother?

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
Yes, Helen, you might be my daughter-in-law:
God shield you mean it not! daughter and mother
So strive upon your pulse. What, pale again?
My fear hath catch'd your fondness: now I see
The mystery of your loneliness, and find
Your salt tears' head: now to all sense 'tis gross
You love my son; invention is ashamed,
Against the proclamation of thy passion,
To say thou dost not: therefore tell me true;
But tell me then, 'tis so; for, look thy cheeks
Confess it, th' one to th' other; and thine eyes
See it so grossly shown in thy behaviors
That in their kind they speak it: only sin
And hellish obstinacy tie thy tongue,
That truth should be suspected. Speak, is't so?
If it be so, you have wound a goodly clew;
If it be not, forswear't: howe'er, I charge thee,
As heaven shall work in me for thine avail,
Tell me truly.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: Good madam, pardon me!

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Do you love my son?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: Your pardon, noble mistress!

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Love you my son?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: Do not you love him, madam?

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
Go not about; my love hath in't a bond,
Whereof the world takes note: come, come, disclose
The state of your affection; for your passions
Have to the full appeach'd.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
Go not about; my love hath in't a bond,
Whereof the world takes note: come, come, disclose
The state of your affection; for your passions
Have to the full appeach'd.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Then, I confess,
Here on my knee, before high heaven and you,
That before you, and next unto high heaven,
I love your son.
My friends were poor, but honest; so's my love:
Be not offended; for it hurts not him
That he is loved of me: I follow him not
By any token of presumptuous suit;
Nor would I have him till I do deserve him;
Yet never know how that desert should be.
I know I love in vain, strive against hope;
Yet in this captious and intenible sieve
I still pour in the waters of my love
And lack not to lose still: thus, Indian-like,
Religious in mine error, I adore
The sun, that looks upon his worshipper,
But knows of him no more. My dearest madam,
Let not your hate encounter with my love
For loving where you do: but if yourself,
Whose aged honour cites a virtuous youth,
Did ever in so true a flame of liking
Wish chastely and love dearly, that your Dian
Was both herself and love: O, then, give pity
To her, whose state is such that cannot choose
But lend and give where she is sure to lose;
That seeks not to find that her search implies,
But riddle-like lives sweetly where she dies!

Yet never know how that desert should be.
I know I love in vain, strive against hope;
Yet in this captious and intenible sieve
I still pour in the waters of my love
And lack not to lose still: thus, Indian-like,
Religious in mine error, I adore
The sun, that looks upon his worshipper,
But knows of him no more. My dearest madam,
Let not your hate encounter with my love
For loving where you do: but if yourself,
Whose aged honour cites a virtuous youth,
Did ever in so true a flame of liking
Wish chastely and love dearly, that your Dian
Was both herself and love: O, then, give pity
To her, whose state is such that cannot choose
But lend and give where she is sure to lose;
That seeks not to find that her search implies,
But riddle-like lives sweetly where she dies!

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Then, I confess,
Here on my knee, before high heaven and you,
That before you, and next unto high heaven,
I love your son.
My friends were poor, but honest; so's my love:
Be not offended; for it hurts not him
That he is loved of me: I follow him not
By any token of presumptuous suit;
Nor would I have him till I do deserve him;
Yet never know how that desert should be.
I know I love in vain, strive against hope;
Yet in this captious and intenible sieve

But riddle-like lives sweetly where she dies!

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
Had you not lately an intent,—speak truly,—
To go to Paris?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: Madam, I had.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Wherefore? tell true.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Wherefore? tell true.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
I will tell truth; by grace itself I swear.
You know my father left me some prescriptions
Of rare and proved effects, such as his reading
And manifest experience had collected
For general sovereignty; and that he will'd me
In heedfull'st reservation to bestow them,
As notes whose faculties inclusive were
More than they were in note: amongst the rest,
There is a remedy, approved, set down,
To cure the desperate languishings whereof
The king is render'd lost.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
This was your motive
For Paris, was it? speak.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
This was your motive
For Paris, was it? speak.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
My lord your son made me to think of this;
Else Paris and the medicine and the king
Had from the conversation of my thoughts
Haply been absent then.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
My lord your son made me to think of this;
Else Paris and the medicine and the king
Had from the conversation of my thoughts
Haply been absent then.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
But think you, Helen,
If you should tender your supposed aid,
He would receive it? he and his physicians
Are of a mind; he, that they cannot help him,
They, that they cannot help: how shall they credit
A poor unlearned virgin, when the schools,
Embowell'd of their doctrine, have left off
The danger to itself?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
There's something in't,
More than my father's skill, which was the greatest
Of his profession, that his good receipt
Shall for my legacy be sanctified
By the luckiest stars in heaven: and, would your honour
But give me leave to try success, I'ld venture
The well-lost life of mine on his grace's cure
By such a day and hour.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
There's something in't,

The well-lost life of mine on his grace's cure
By such a day and hour.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Dost thou believe't?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: Ay, madam, knowingly.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
Why, Helen, thou shalt have my leave and love,
Means and attendants and my loving greetings
To those of mine in court: I'll stay at home
And pray God's blessing into thy attempt:
Be gone to-morrow; and be sure of this,
What I can help thee to thou shalt not miss.

Flourish of cornets. Enter the KING, attended with divers young Lords taking leave for the Florentine war; BERTRAM, and PAROLLES
Flourish of cornets. Enter the KING, attended with divers young Lords taking leave for the Florentine war; BERTRAM, and PAROLLES

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Farewell, young lords; these warlike principles
Do not throw from you: and you, my lords, farewell:
Share the advice betwixt you; if both gain all,
The gift doth stretch itself as 'tis received,
And is enough for both.

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text:
'Tis our hope, sir,
After well enter'd soldiers, to return
And find your grace in health.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
No, no, it cannot be; and yet my heart
Will not confess he owes the malady
That doth my life besiege. Farewell, young lords;
Whether I live or die, be you the sons
Of worthy Frenchmen: let higher Italy,—
Those bated that inherit but the fall
Of the last monarchy,—see that you come
Not to woo honour, but to wed it; when
The bravest questant shrinks, find what you seek,
That fame may cry you loud: I say, farewell.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text: Health, at your bidding, serve your majesty!

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Those girls of Italy, take heed of them:
They say, our French lack language to deny,
If they demand: beware of being captives,
Before you serve.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: BOTH
sentences.text: Our hearts receive your warnings.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text: Farewell. Come hither to me.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text: Farewell. Come hither to me.
Exit, attended

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text: O, my sweet lord, that you will stay behind us!

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: 'Tis not his fault, the spark.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text: O, 'tis brave wars!

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Most admirable: I have seen those wars.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
I am commanded here, and kept a coil with
'Too young' and 'the next year' and ''tis too early.'

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: An thy mind stand to't, boy, steal away bravely.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
I shall stay here the forehorse to a smock,
Creaking my shoes on the plain masonry,
Till honour be bought up and no sword worn
But one to dance with! By heaven, I'll steal away.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text: There's honour in the theft.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Commit it, count.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text: I am your accessary; and so, farewell.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: I grow to you, and our parting is a tortured body.

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text: Farewell, captain.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text: Sweet Monsieur Parolles!

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
Noble heroes, my sword and yours are kin. Good
sparks and lustrous, a word, good metals: you shall
find in the regiment of the Spinii one Captain
Spurio, with his cicatrice, an emblem of war, here
on his sinister cheek; it was this very sword
entrenched it: say to him, I live; and observe his
reports for me.

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text: We shall, noble captain.
Exeunt Lords

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Mars dote on you for his novices! what will ye do?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Mars dote on you for his novices! what will ye do?

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: Stay: the king.
Re-enter KING. BERTRAM and PAROLLES retire
Re-enter KING. BERTRAM and PAROLLES retire

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
[To BERTRAM] Use a more spacious ceremony to the
noble lords; you have restrained yourself within the
list of too cold an adieu: be more expressive to
them: for they wear themselves in the cap of the
time, there do muster true gait, eat, speak, and
move under the influence of the most received star;
and though the devil lead the measure, such are to
be followed: after them, and take a more dilated
farewell.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: And I will do so.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
Worthy fellows; and like to prove most sinewy
sword-men.
Exeunt BERTRAM and PAROLLES
Enter LAFEU

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text: [Kneeling] Pardon, my lord, for me and for my tidings.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text: I'll fee thee to stand up.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Then here's a man stands, that has brought his pardon.
I would you had kneel'd, my lord, to ask me mercy,
And that at my bidding you could so stand up.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Then here's a man stands, that has brought his pardon.
I would you had kneel'd, my lord, to ask me mercy,
And that at my bidding you could so stand up.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
I would I had; so I had broke thy pate,
And ask'd thee mercy for't.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
I would I had; so I had broke thy pate,
And ask'd thee mercy for't.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Good faith, across: but, my good lord 'tis thus;
Will you be cured of your infirmity?

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Good faith, across: but, my good lord 'tis thus;
Will you be cured of your infirmity?

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text: No.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
O, will you eat no grapes, my royal fox?
Yes, but you will my noble grapes, an if
My royal fox could reach them: I have seen a medicine
That's able to breathe life into a stone,
Quicken a rock, and make you dance canary
With spritely fire and motion; whose simple touch,
Is powerful to araise King Pepin, nay,
To give great Charlemain a pen in's hand,
And write to her a love-line.

Yes, but you will my noble grapes, an if
My royal fox could reach them: I have seen a medicine
That's able to breathe life into a stone,
Quicken a rock, and make you dance canary
With spritely fire and motion; whose simple touch,
Is powerful to araise King Pepin, nay,
To give great Charlemain a pen in's hand,
And write to her a love-line.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text: What 'her' is this?

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Why, Doctor She: my lord, there's one arrived,
If you will see her: now, by my faith and honour,
If seriously I may convey my thoughts
In this my light deliverance, I have spoke
With one that, in her sex, her years, profession,
Wisdom and constancy, hath amazed me more
Than I dare blame my weakness: will you see her
For that is her demand, and know her business?
That done, laugh well at me.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Now, good Lafeu,
Bring in the admiration; that we with thee
May spend our wonder too, or take off thine
By wondering how thou took'st it.

sentences.speaker: KING

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Nay, I'll fit you,
And not be all day neither.
Exit

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text: Thus he his special nothing ever prologues.
Re-enter LAFEU, with HELENA

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text: Nay, come your ways.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text: This haste hath wings indeed.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Nay, come your ways:
This is his majesty; say your mind to him:
A traitor you do look like; but such traitors
His majesty seldom fears: I am Cressid's uncle,
That dare leave two together; fare you well.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text: Now, fair one, does your business follow us?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
HELENA
HELENA
KING
sentences.text: I knew him.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
The rather will I spare my praises towards him:
Knowing him is enough. On's bed of death
Many receipts he gave me: chiefly one.
Which, as the dearest issue of his practise,
And of his old experience the oily darling,
He bade me store up, as a triple eye,
Safer than mine own two, more dear; I have so;
And hearing your high majesty is touch'd
With that malignant cause wherein the honour
Of my dear father's gift stands chief in power,
I come to tender it and my appliance
With all bound humbleness.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
We thank you, maiden;
But may not be so credulous of cure,
When our most learned doctors leave us and
The congregated college have concluded
That labouring art can never ransom nature
From her inaidible estate; I say we must not
So stain our judgment, or corrupt our hope,
To prostitute our past-cure malady
To empirics, or to dissever so
Our great self and our credit, to esteem
A senseless help when help past sense we deem.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
My duty then shall pay me for my pains:
I will no more enforce mine office on you.
Humbly entreating from your royal thoughts
A modest one, to bear me back again.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
I cannot give thee less, to be call'd grateful:
Thou thought'st to help me; and such thanks I give
As one near death to those that wish him live:
But what at full I know, thou know'st no part,
I knowing all my peril, thou no art.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
What I can do can do no hurt to try,
Since you set up your rest 'gainst remedy.
He that of greatest works is finisher
Oft does them by the weakest minister:
So holy writ in babes hath judgment shown,
When judges have been babes; great floods have flown
From simple sources, and great seas have dried
When miracles have by the greatest been denied.
Oft expectation fails and most oft there
Where most it promises, and oft it hits
Where hope is coldest and despair most fits.

sentences.speaker: KING
KING
KING
HELENA
sentences.text:
Inspired merit so by breath is barr'd:
It is not so with Him that all things knows
As 'tis with us that square our guess by shows;
But most it is presumption in us when
The help of heaven we count the act of men.
Dear sir, to my endeavours give consent;
Of heaven, not me, make an experiment.
I am not an impostor that proclaim
Myself against the level of mine aim;
But know I think and think I know most sure
My art is not past power nor you past cure.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Are thou so confident? within what space
Hopest thou my cure?

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Are thou so confident? within what space
Hopest thou my cure?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
The great'st grace lending grace
Ere twice the horses of the sun shall bring
Their fiery torcher his diurnal ring,
Ere twice in murk and occidental damp
Moist Hesperus hath quench'd his sleepy lamp,
Or four and twenty times the pilot's glass
Hath told the thievish minutes how they pass,
What is infirm from your sound parts shall fly,
Health shall live free and sickness freely die.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Upon thy certainty and confidence
What darest thou venture?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Tax of impudence,
A strumpet's boldness, a divulged shame
Traduced by odious ballads: my maiden's name
Sear'd otherwise; nay, worse—if worse—extended
With vilest torture let my life be ended.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Tax of impudence,
A strumpet's boldness, a divulged shame
Traduced by odious ballads: my maiden's name
Sear'd otherwise; nay, worse—if worse—extended
With vilest torture let my life be ended.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Methinks in thee some blessed spirit doth speak
His powerful sound within an organ weak:
And what impossibility would slay
In common sense, sense saves another way.
Thy life is dear; for all that life can rate
Worth name of life in thee hath estimate,
Youth, beauty, wisdom, courage, all
That happiness and prime can happy call:
Thou this to hazard needs must intimate
Skill infinite or monstrous desperate.
Sweet practiser, thy physic I will try,
That ministers thine own death if I die.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
If I break time, or flinch in property
Of what I spoke, unpitied let me die,
And well deserved: not helping, death's my fee;
But, if I help, what do you promise me?

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text: Make thy demand.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: But will you make it even?

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text: Ay, by my sceptre and my hopes of heaven.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Then shalt thou give me with thy kingly hand
What husband in thy power I will command:
Exempted be from me the arrogance
To choose from forth the royal blood of France,
My low and humble name to propagate
With any branch or image of thy state;
But such a one, thy vassal, whom I know
Is free for me to ask, thee to bestow.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Here is my hand ; the premises observed,
Thy will by my performance shall be served:
So make the choice of thy own time, for I,
Thy resolved patient, on thee still rely.
More should I question thee, and more I must,
Though more to know could not be more to trust,
From whence thou camest, how tended on: but rest
Unquestion'd welcome and undoubted blest.
Give me some help here, ho! If thou proceed
As high as word, my deed shall match thy meed.

From whence thou camest, how tended on: but rest
Unquestion'd welcome and undoubted blest.
Give me some help here, ho! If thou proceed
As high as word, my deed shall match thy meed.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Here is my hand ; the premises observed,
Thy will by my performance shall be served:
So make the choice of thy own time, for I,
Thy resolved patient, on thee still rely.
More should I question thee, and more I must,
Though more to know could not be more to trust,

Come on, sir; I shall now put you to the height of
your breeding.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS

know my business is but to the court.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
To the court! why, what place make you special,
when you put off that with such contempt? But to the
court!

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
Truly, madam, if God have lent a man any manners, he
may easily put it off at court: he that cannot make
a leg, put off's cap, kiss his hand and say nothing,
has neither leg, hands, lip, nor cap; and indeed
such a fellow, to say precisely, were not for the
court; but for me, I have an answer will serve all
men.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
Marry, that's a bountiful answer that fits all
questions.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
It is like a barber's chair that fits all buttocks,
the pin-buttock, the quatch-buttock, the brawn
buttock, or any buttock.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Will your answer serve fit to all questions?

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
As fit as ten groats is for the hand of an attorney,
as your French crown for your taffeta punk, as Tib's
rush for Tom's forefinger, as a pancake for Shrove
Tuesday, a morris for May-day, as the nail to his
hole, the cuckold to his horn, as a scolding quean
to a wrangling knave, as the nun's lip to the
friar's mouth, nay, as the pudding to his skin.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS

Have you, I say, an answer of such fitness for all
questions?

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
From below your duke to beneath your constable, it
will fit any question.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
It must be an answer of most monstrous size that
must fit all demands.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
But a trifle neither, in good faith, if the learned
should speak truth of it: here it is, and all that
belongs to't. Ask me if I am a courtier: it shall
do you no harm to learn.

pray you, sir, are you a courtier?

sentences.speaker: CLOWN

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: Sir, I am a poor friend of yours, that loves you.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text: O Lord, sir! Thick, thick, spare not me.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: I think, sir, you can eat none of this homely meat.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text: O Lord, sir! Nay, put me to't, I warrant you.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text: You were lately whipped, sir, as I think.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text: O Lord, sir! spare not me.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS
sentences.text:
Do you cry, 'O Lord, sir!' at your whipping, and
'spare not me?' Indeed your 'O Lord, sir!' is very
sequent to your whipping: you would answer very well
to a whipping, if you were but bound to't.

I play the noble housewife with the time

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text: O Lord, sir! why, there't serves well again.

Commend me to my kinsmen and my son:
This is not much.

sentences.speaker: COUNTESS

sentences.speaker: CLOWN

Enter BERTRAM, LAFEU, and PAROLLES

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
They say miracles are past; and we have our
philosophical persons, to make modern and familiar,
things supernatural and causeless. Hence is it that
we make trifles of terrors, ensconcing ourselves
into seeming knowledge, when we should submit
ourselves to an unknown fear.

Why, 'tis the rarest argument of wonder that hath
shot out in our latter times.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: And so 'tis.

Go, call before me all the lords in court.
Sit, my preserver, by thy patient's side;
And with this healthful hand, whose banish'd sense
Thou hast repeal'd, a second time receive
The confirmation of my promised gift,
Which but attends thy naming.

sentences.speaker: KING

Fair maid, send forth thine eye : this youthful parcel
Of noble bachelors stand at my bestowing,
O'er whom both sovereign power and father's voice
I have to use: thy frank election make;
Thou hast power to choose, and they none to forsake.

Fall, when Love please! marry, to each, but one!

Peruse them well:
Not one of those but had a noble father.

Gentlemen,
Heaven hath through me restored the king to health.

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: We understand it, and thank heaven for you.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
I am a simple maid, and therein wealthiest,
That I protest I simply am a maid.
Please it your majesty, I have done already:
The blushes in my cheeks thus whisper me,
'We blush that thou shouldst choose; but, be refused,
Let the white death sit on thy cheek for ever;
We'll ne'er come there again.'

Who shuns thy love shuns all his love in me.

Now, Dian, from thy altar do I fly,
And to imperial Love, that god most high,
Do my sighs stream. Sir, will you hear my suit?

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: Thanks, sir; all the rest is mute.

sentences.speaker: HELENA

I had rather be in this choice than throw ames-ace
for my life.

sentences.speaker: HELENA

The honour, sir, that flames in your fair eyes,
Before I speak, too threateningly replies:
Love make your fortunes twenty times above
Her that so wishes and her humble love!

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text: No better, if you please.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
My wish receive,
Which great Love grant! and so, I take my leave.

In such a business give me leave to use
The help of mine own eyes.

Know'st thou not, Bertram,
What she has done for me?

Yes, my good lord;
But never hope to know why I should marry her.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text: Thou know'st she has raised me from my sickly bed.

But follows it, my lord, to bring me down
Must answer for your raising? I know her well:
She had her breeding at my father's charge.
A poor physician's daughter my wife! Disdain
Rather corrupt me ever!

All that is virtuous, save what thou dislikest,
A poor physician's daughter, thou dislikest
Of virtue for the name: but do not so:
From lowest place when virtuous things proceed,
The place is dignified by the doer's deed:
Where great additions swell's, and virtue none,
It is a dropsied honour. Good alone
Is good without a name. Vileness is so:
The property by what it is should go,
Not by the title. She is young, wise, fair;
In these to nature she's immediate heir,
And these breed honour: that is honour's scorn,
Which challenges itself as honour's born
And is not like the sire: honours thrive,
When rather from our acts we them derive
Than our foregoers: the mere word's a slave
Debauched on every tomb, on every grave
A lying trophy, and as oft is dumb
Where dust and damn'd oblivion is the tomb
Of honour'd bones indeed. What should be said?
If thou canst like this creature as a maid,
I can create the rest: virtue and she
Is her own dower; honour and wealth from me.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: I cannot love her, nor will strive to do't.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Thou wrong'st thyself, if thou shouldst strive to
choose.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
That you are well restored, my lord, I'm glad:
Let the rest go.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
That you are well restored, my lord, I'm glad:
Let the rest go.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
My honour's at the stake; which to defeat,
I must produce my power. Here, take her hand,
Proud scornful boy, unworthy this good gift;
That dost in vile misprision shackle up
My love and her desert; that canst not dream,
We, poising us in her defective scale,
Shall weigh thee to the beam; that wilt not know,
It is in us to plant thine honour where
We please to have it grow. Cheque thy contempt:
Obey our will, which travails in thy good:
Believe not thy disdain, but presently
Do thine own fortunes that obedient right
Which both thy duty owes and our power claims;
Or I will throw thee from my care for ever
Into the staggers and the careless lapse
Of youth and ignorance; both my revenge and hate
Loosing upon thee, in the name of justice,
Without all terms of pity. Speak; thine answer.

Believe not thy disdain, but presently
Do thine own fortunes that obedient right
Which both thy duty owes and our power claims;
Or I will throw thee from my care for ever
Into the staggers and the careless lapse
Of youth and ignorance; both my revenge and hate
Loosing upon thee, in the name of justice,
Without all terms of pity. Speak; thine answer.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
My honour's at the stake; which to defeat,
I must produce my power. Here, take her hand,
Proud scornful boy, unworthy this good gift;
That dost in vile misprision shackle up
My love and her desert; that canst not dream,
We, poising us in her defective scale,
Shall weigh thee to the beam; that wilt not know,
It is in us to plant thine honour where
We please to have it grow. Cheque thy contempt:
Obey our will, which travails in thy good:
Believe not thy disdain, but presently
Do thine own fortunes that obedient right
Which both thy duty owes and our power claims;
Or I will throw thee from my care for ever
Into the staggers and the careless lapse
Of youth and ignorance; both my revenge and hate
Loosing upon thee, in the name of justice,
Without all terms of pity. Speak; thine answer.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
Pardon, my gracious lord; for I submit
My fancy to your eyes: when I consider
What great creation and what dole of honour
Flies where you bid it, I find that she, which late
Was in my nobler thoughts most base, is now
The praised of the king; who, so ennobled,
Is as 'twere born so.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Take her by the hand,
And tell her she is thine: to whom I promise
A counterpoise, if not to thy estate
A balance more replete.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: I take her hand.

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
Good fortune and the favour of the king
Smile upon this contract; whose ceremony
Shall seem expedient on the now-born brief,
And be perform'd to-night: the solemn feast
Shall more attend upon the coming space,
Expecting absent friends. As thou lovest her,
Thy love's to me religious; else, does err.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text: [Advancing] Do you hear, monsieur? a word with you.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text: [Advancing] Do you hear, monsieur? a word with you.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Your pleasure, sir?

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Your lord and master did well to make his
recantation.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Recantation! My lord! my master!

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Recantation! My lord! my master!

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Recantation! My lord! my master!

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text: Ay; is it not a language I speak?

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text: Ay; is it not a language I speak?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
A most harsh one, and not to be understood without
bloody succeeding. My master!

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
A most harsh one, and not to be understood without
bloody succeeding. My master!

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text: Are you companion to the Count Rousillon?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: To any count, to all counts, to what is man.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
To what is count's man: count's master is of
another style.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
To what is count's man: count's master is of
another style.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
You are too old, sir; let it satisfy you, you are too
old.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
You are too old, sir; let it satisfy you, you are too
old.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
I must tell thee, sirrah, I write man; to which
title age cannot bring thee.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
I must tell thee, sirrah, I write man; to which
title age cannot bring thee.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: What I dare too well do, I dare not do.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
I did think thee, for two ordinaries, to be a pretty
wise fellow; thou didst make tolerable vent of thy
travel; it might pass: yet the scarfs and the
bannerets about thee did manifoldly dissuade me from
believing thee a vessel of too great a burthen. I
have now found thee; when I lose thee again, I care
not: yet art thou good for nothing but taking up; and
that thou't scarce worth.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Hadst thou not the privilege of antiquity upon thee,—

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Do not plunge thyself too far in anger, lest thou
hasten thy trial; which if—Lord have mercy on thee
for a hen! So, my good window of lattice, fare thee
well: thy casement I need not open, for I look
through thee. Give me thy hand.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: My lord, you give me most egregious indignity.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text: Ay, with all my heart; and thou art worthy of it.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: I have not, my lord, deserved it.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Yes, good faith, every dram of it; and I will not
bate thee a scruple.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Well, I shall be wiser.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Even as soon as thou canst, for thou hast to pull at
a smack o' the contrary. If ever thou be'st bound
in thy scarf and beaten, thou shalt find what it is
to be proud of thy bondage. I have a desire to hold
my acquaintance with thee, or rather my knowledge,
that I may say in the default, he is a man I know.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: My lord, you do me most insupportable vexation.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
I would it were hell-pains for thy sake, and my poor
doing eternal: for doing I am past: as I will by
thee, in what motion age will give me leave.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
I would it were hell-pains for thy sake, and my poor
doing eternal: for doing I am past: as I will by
thee, in what motion age will give me leave.
Exit

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
Well, thou hast a son shall take this disgrace off
me; scurvy, old, filthy, scurvy lord! Well, I must
be patient; there is no fettering of authority.
I'll beat him, by my life, if I can meet him with
any convenience, an he were double and double a
lord. I'll have no more pity of his age than I
would of —I'll beat him, an if I could but meet him
again.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Sirrah, your lord and master's married; there's news
for you: you have a new mistress.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Sirrah, your lord and master's married; there's news
for you: you have a new mistress.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Sirrah, your lord and master's married; there's news
for you: you have a new mistress.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
I most unfeignedly beseech your lordship to make
some reservation of your wrongs: he is my good
lord: whom I serve above is my master.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
I most unfeignedly beseech your lordship to make
some reservation of your wrongs: he is my good
lord: whom I serve above is my master.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:

some reservation of your wrongs: he is my good

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text: Who? God?

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text: Who? God?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Ay, sir.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
The devil it is that's thy master. Why dost thou
garter up thy arms o' this fashion? dost make hose of
sleeves? do other servants so? Thou wert best set
thy lower part where thy nose stands. By mine
honour, if I were but two hours younger, I'ld beat
thee: methinks, thou art a general offence, and
every man should beat thee: I think thou wast
created for men to breathe themselves upon thee.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: This is hard and undeserved measure, my lord.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Go to, sir; you were beaten in Italy for picking a
kernel out of a pomegranate; you are a vagabond and
no true traveller: you are more saucy with lords
and honourable personages than the commission of your
birth and virtue gives you heraldry. You are not
worth another word, else I'ld call you knave. I leave
you.

no true traveller: you are more saucy with lords
and honourable personages than the commission of your
birth and virtue gives you heraldry. You are not
worth another word, else I'ld call you knave. I leave
you.

let it be concealed awhile.

Re-enter BERTRAM

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: Undone, and forfeited to cares for ever!

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: What's the matter, sweet-heart?

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
Although before the solemn priest I have sworn,
I will not bed her.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: What, what, sweet-heart?

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
O my Parolles, they have married me!
I'll to the Tuscan wars, and never bed her.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
O my Parolles, they have married me!
I'll to the Tuscan wars, and never bed her.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
France is a dog-hole, and it no more merits
The tread of a man's foot: to the wars!

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
France is a dog-hole, and it no more merits
The tread of a man's foot: to the wars!

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
There's letters from my mother: what the import is,
I know not yet.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
There's letters from my mother: what the import is,
I know not yet.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
Ay, that would be known. To the wars, my boy, to the
wars!
He wears his honour in a box unseen,
That hugs his kicky-wicky here at home,
Spending his manly marrow in her arms,
Which should sustain the bound and high curvet
Of Mars's fiery steed. To other regions
France is a stable; we that dwell in't jades;
Therefore, to the war!

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
It shall be so: I'll send her to my house,
Acquaint my mother with my hate to her,
And wherefore I am fled; write to the king
That which I durst not speak; his present gift
Shall furnish me to those Italian fields,
Where noble fellows strike: war is no strife
To the dark house and the detested wife.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Will this capriccio hold in thee? art sure?

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
Go with me to my chamber, and advise me.
I'll send her straight away: to-morrow
I'll to the wars, she to her single sorrow.

sentences.text:
Why, these balls bound; there's noise in it. 'Tis hard:
A young man married is a man that's marr'd:
Therefore away, and leave her bravely; go:
The king has done you wrong: but, hush, 'tis so.

Enter HELENA and Clown

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: My mother greets me kindly; is she well?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: My mother greets me kindly; is she well?

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
She is not well; but yet she has her health: she's
very merry; but yet she is not well: but thanks be
given, she's very well and wants nothing i', the
world; but yet she is not well.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
If she be very well, what does she ail, that she's
not very well?

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text: Truly, she's very well indeed, but for two things.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: What two things?

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
One, that she's not in heaven, whither God send her
quickly! the other that she's in earth, from whence
God send her quickly!

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:

Enter PAROLLES

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Bless you, my fortunate lady!

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
I hope, sir, I have your good will to have mine own
good fortunes.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
You had my prayers to lead them on; and to keep them
on, have them still. O, my knave, how does my old lady?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
You had my prayers to lead them on; and to keep them
on, have them still. O, my knave, how does my old lady?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
You had my prayers to lead them on; and to keep them
on, have them still. O, my knave, how does my old lady?

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
So that you had her wrinkles and I her money,
I would she did as you say.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Why, I say nothing.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
Marry, you are the wiser man; for many a man's
tongue shakes out his master's undoing: to say
nothing, to do nothing, to know nothing, and to have
nothing, is to be a great part of your title; which
is within a very little of nothing.

sentences.text:
You should have said, sir, before a knave thou'rt a
knave; that's, before me thou'rt a knave: this had
been truth, sir.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Go to, thou art a witty fool; I have found thee.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Go to, thou art a witty fool; I have found thee.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Go to, thou art a witty fool; I have found thee.

sentences.speaker: CLOWN
sentences.text:
Did you find me in yourself, sir? or were you
taught to find me? The search, sir, was profitable;
and much fool may you find in you, even to the
world's pleasure and the increase of laughter.

HELENA
sentences.text: What's his will else?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
That you will take your instant leave o' the king
And make this haste as your own good proceeding,
Strengthen'd with what apology you think
May make it probable need.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
That you will take your instant leave o' the king
And make this haste as your own good proceeding,
Strengthen'd with what apology you think
May make it probable need.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: What more commands he?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
That, having this obtain'd, you presently
Attend his further pleasure.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: In every thing I wait upon his will.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: I shall report it so.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: I pray you.
Exit PAROLLES

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: Come, sirrah.
Exeunt
Enter LAFEU and BERTRAM

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text: But I hope your lordship thinks not him a soldier.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: Yes, my lord, and of very valiant approof.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text: You have it from his own deliverance.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: And by other warranted testimony.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Then my dial goes not true: I took this lark for a
bunting.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
Then my dial goes not true: I took this lark for a
bunting.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
I do assure you, my lord, he is very great in
knowledge and accordingly valiant.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
I have then sinned against his experience and
transgressed against his valour; and my state that
way is dangerous, since I cannot yet find in my
heart to repent. Here he comes: I pray you, make
us friends; I will pursue the amity.

Enter PAROLLES

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: [To BERTRAM] These things shall be done, sir.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text: Pray you, sir, who's his tailor?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Sir?

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
O, I know him well, I, sir; he, sir, 's a good
workman, a very good tailor.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
O, I know him well, I, sir; he, sir, 's a good
workman, a very good tailor.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: [Aside to PAROLLES] Is she gone to the king?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: She is.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: Will she away to-night?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: As you'll have her.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
I have writ my letters, casketed my treasure,
Given order for our horses; and to-night,
When I should take possession of the bride,
End ere I do begin.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
A good traveller is something at the latter end of a
dinner; but one that lies three thirds and uses a
known truth to pass a thousand nothings with, should
be once heard and thrice beaten. God save you, captain.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
Is there any unkindness between my lord and you,
monsieur?

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text:
I know not how I have deserved to run into my lord's
displeasure.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
You have made shift to run into 't, boots and spurs
and all, like him that leaped into the custard; and
out of it you'll run again, rather than suffer
question for your residence.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: It may be you have mistaken him, my lord.

sentences.speaker: LAFEU
sentences.text:
And shall do so ever, though I took him at 's
prayers. Fare you well, my lord; and believe this
of me, there can be no kernel in this light nut; the
soul of this man is his clothes. Trust him not in
matter of heavy consequence; I have kept of them
tame, and know their natures. Farewell, monsieur:
I have spoken better of you than you have or will to
deserve at my hand; but we must do good against evil.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: An idle lord. I swear.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: I think so.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Why, do you not know him?

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
Yes, I do know him well, and common speech
Gives him a worthy pass. Here comes my clog.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
Yes, I do know him well, and common speech
Gives him a worthy pass. Here comes my clog.
Enter HELENA

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
I have, sir, as I was commanded from you,
Spoke with the king and have procured his leave
For present parting; only he desires
Some private speech with you.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
I have, sir, as I was commanded from you,
Spoke with the king and have procured his leave
For present parting; only he desires
Some private speech with you.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
I shall obey his will.
You must not marvel, Helen, at my course,
Which holds not colour with the time, nor does
The ministration and required office
On my particular. Prepared I was not
For such a business; therefore am I found
So much unsettled: this drives me to entreat you
That presently you take our way for home;
And rather muse than ask why I entreat you,
For my respects are better than they seem
And my appointments have in them a need
Greater than shows itself at the first view
To you that know them not. This to my mother:

Greater than shows itself at the first view
To you that know them not. This to my mother:

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
I shall obey his will.
You must not marvel, Helen, at my course,
Which holds not colour with the time, nor does
The ministration and required office
On my particular. Prepared I was not
For such a business; therefore am I found
So much unsettled: this drives me to entreat you
That presently you take our way for home;
And rather muse than ask why I entreat you,
For my respects are better than they seem
And my appointments have in them a need
Greater than shows itself at the first view
To you that know them not. This to my mother:

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
'Twill be two days ere I shall see you, so
I leave you to your wisdom.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Sir, I can nothing say,
But that I am your most obedient servant.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: Come, come, no more of that.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
And ever shall
With true observance seek to eke out that
Wherein toward me my homely stars have fail'd
To equal my great fortune.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
Let that go:
My haste is very great: farewell; hie home.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
Let that go:
My haste is very great: farewell; hie home.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: Pray, sir, your pardon.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: Well, what would you say?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
I am not worthy of the wealth I owe,
Nor dare I say 'tis mine, and yet it is;
But, like a timorous thief, most fain would steal
What law does vouch mine own.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: What would you have?

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text:
Something; and scarce so much: nothing, indeed.
I would not tell you what I would, my lord:
Faith yes;
Strangers and foes do sunder, and not kiss.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: I pray you, stay not, but in haste to horse.

sentences.speaker: HELENA
sentences.text: I shall not break your bidding, good my lord.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: Where are my other men, monsieur? Farewell.

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text: Where are my other men, monsieur? Farewell.
Exit HELENA

sentences.speaker: BERTRAM
sentences.text:
Go thou toward home; where I will never come
Whilst I can shake my sword or hear the drum.
Away, and for our flight.

sentences.speaker: PAROLLES
sentences.text: Bravely, coragio!
Exeunt
Flourish. Enter the DUKE of Florence attended; the two Frenchmen, with a troop of soldiers.
Flourish. Enter the DUKE of Florence attended; the two Frenchmen, with a troop of soldiers.

sentences.speaker: DUKE
sentences.text:
So that from point to point now have you heard
The fundamental reasons of this war,
Whose great decision hath much blood let forth
And more thirsts after.

sentences.speaker: DUKE
sentences.text:
So that from point to point now have you heard
The fundamental reasons of this war,
Whose great decision hath much blood let forth
And more thirsts after.

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text:
Holy seems the quarrel
Upon your grace's part; black and fearful
On the opposer.

sentences.speaker: FIRST LORD
sentences.text:
Holy seems the quarrel
Upon your grace's part; black and fearful
On the opposer.

sentences.speaker: DUKE
sentences.text:
Therefore we marvel much our cousin France
Would in so just a business shut his bosom
Against our borrowing prayers.

sentences.speaker: SECOND LORD
sentences.text:
Good my lord,
The reasons of our state I cannot yield,
But like a common and an outward man,
That the great figure of a council frames
By self-unable motion : therefore dare not
Say what I think of it, since I have found
Myself in my incertain grounds to fail
As often as I guess'd.
